title: SEGMENT 9 — Splice-switching ASOs and the nusinersen precedent (Spinraza for SMA)

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Splice-switching ASOs and the nusinersen precedent (Spinraza for SMA)
I want to dwell for an entire segment on one specific class of ASOs — the splice-modulating ones — and on one specific drug that had been approved by the FDA two years before Mila's treatment began.
Because that one drug, and its approval, is what made milasen possible.
Without it, the entire milasen story almost certainly does not happen.
The drug is called nusinersen, brand name Spinraza.
It treats a disease called spinal muscular atrophy, or SMA, a devastating motor neuron disease that, in its most severe infantile form, used to kill affected babies before their second birthday.
SMA is caused by loss of a gene called SMN1, which makes a protein called survival motor neuron, essential for the health of the motor neurons that drive your muscles.
Without SMN1, motor neurons in the spinal cord die, and the affected child progressively loses the ability to move, breathe, and swallow.
Here is the molecular twist that made SMA treatable.
Humans have a second, very similar gene called SMN2, which arose from a duplication of SMN1 sometime in primate evolution.
SMN2 makes almost the same protein as SMN1 — but for reasons of one single-base difference in its sequence, SMN2's pre-mRNA is mostly spliced incorrectly.
The spliceosome skips exon 7 of SMN2 most of the time, producing a truncated, unstable protein that doesn't work.
Only about ten to fifteen percent of SMN2 transcripts are spliced correctly to include exon 7 and produce functional protein.
That is not enough, by itself, to compensate for the loss of SMN1.

But — and this is the brilliant insight — if you could shift the splicing of SMN2 to include exon 7 more efficiently, you could produce more functional protein from the gene the patient already has, and partially rescue the disease.
That is exactly what nusinersen does.
It is a splice-modulating ASO targeted to a specific intronic regulatory element in SMN2 — an element that normally promotes the skipping of exon 7.
By binding to that element and masking it, nusinersen prevents the skipping.
Exon 7 gets included more often.
More functional SMN protein gets made.
The motor neurons of the treated patient do better.
The disease progresses much more slowly, or in babies treated very early, doesn't progress at all.
Nusinersen was approved by the FDA in December of 2016.
It was the first splice-modulating ASO approved for any human disease, and it was a transformative success.
Babies who would have died now grow into children who can sit up, who can walk, who can attend school.
It was, in every sense, a paradigm shift.
The drug is delivered intrathecally, by spinal injection, every few months for the rest of the patient's life.

Its chemical backbone is exactly the chemistry I described in Segment 8 — phosphorothioate linkages, 2'-O-methoxyethyl sugar modifications, a roughly twenty-base oligonucleotide of carefully chosen sequence.
Here is why nusinersen mattered so much for milasen, beyond just being a scientific success in its own right.
It was the precedent.
It proved several things at once.

It proved that splice-modulating ASOs can work clinically — that the theoretical mechanism of switching a splicing decision by base-pairing actually translates into clinical benefit in a real human disease.
It proved that this specific chemistry — the 2'-MOE phosphorothioate backbone — is safe enough to give to children, repeatedly, for years, by intrathecal injection.
It proved that the route of administration — spinal injection every few months — is tolerated by pediatric patients in clinical practice.
And it proved that the FDA was willing to approve drugs designed against these targets with this chemistry.
In other words, nusinersen built an entire platform.
The chemistry was de-risked.
The delivery route was de-risked.
The mechanism was de-risked.
The regulatory pathway had been blazed.
If, in 2017, you wanted to design a brand-new splice-modulating ASO for a different disease, you did not have to reinvent any of those wheels.

You could pick up the same chemistry, design a new sequence aimed at your new target, deliver it the same way, and have a reasonable expectation that the major engineering questions — safety, deliverability, mechanism — would behave the way they had behaved for nusinersen.
The platform was portable.
The sequence was the variable.
Everything else was a copy.
That is exactly how Yu and his colleagues approached milasen.
They did not have to invent ASO chemistry.
They did not have to invent intrathecal delivery.
They did not have to invent a regulatory pathway.
They had to design a new sequence — a new sticky note for a new word in a new sentence — using the existing platform that nusinersen had validated.
That is a much, much smaller undertaking than developing a drug from scratch.
That is what made one year possible.
I want to name this idea explicitly because it is going to recur as a theme.
In modern drug development, platforms matter more than individual drugs.

When a new platform — a new chemistry, a new delivery route, a new molecular class — is validated for the first time in one disease, it dramatically lowers the barrier to attempting it in other diseases.
mRNA vaccines were a research curiosity for two decades until the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines were authorized, and now mRNA-based therapies are being tested for dozens of conditions.
CAR-T cells took thirty years to reach the first approval, and now CAR-T programs exist for every conceivable cancer.
The first hard climb is the platform climb.
The second climb, for the next disease using the same platform, is much shorter.
Splice-modulating ASOs were validated by nusinersen.
Milasen was the first follow-on after that validation, and the speed of milasen's development is a direct consequence of standing on top of the nusinersen platform.

This is also, by the way, the reason organizations like the n-Lorem Foundation — which we will discuss in Segment 17 — can now contemplate producing custom ASOs for individual patients on a regular basis.
The hard chemistry work has already been done.
The platform is mature.
The only piece that has to be customized for each new patient is the twenty-base sequence of the oligonucleotide itself, which can be redesigned for a new target in a matter of weeks.
Alright.
With nusinersen as our precedent firmly in mind, we are ready to talk about the actual design of milasen.
But first — we have been at this for roughly an hour.
Let's take a fifteen-minute break, get coffee, stretch, and come back ready to walk through the molecular engineering of the drug itself.
